---
title: "new"
output: html_document
date: "2026-03-17"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots



Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.



```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(GGally)
#install.packages("FNN")
library(FNN)
library(pROC)
library(caret)
library(ROCR) 
library(class)      # For KNN implementation
library(corrplot)
```

```{r}

data <- read.csv("student_productivity.csv")
head(data, 5)
```



```{r}

data <- na.omit(data)

# Check dimensions after removing missing values
dim(data)

########################
# 3. Keep useful variables and basic cleaning
########################
# Convert gender to factor
data$gender <- as.factor(data$gender)

# Create a binary variable for logistic regression later
# This is only for the optional classification extension,
# not the main regression question.
data$high_productivity <- ifelse(
  data$productivity_score > median(data$productivity_score),
  "High", "Low"
)
data$high_productivity <- factor(data$high_productivity, levels = c("Low", "High"))

########################
# 4. Research question setup
########################
# Main response variable
y_var <- "productivity_score"

# Main predictor for the core question
x_var <- "study_hours_per_day"

# Additional predictors for multiple regression
# Choose behavioural / study-related variables
mlr_vars <- c(
  "study_hours_per_day",
  "focus_score",
  "attendance_percentage",
  "assignments_completed",
  "sleep_hours",
  "stress_level"
)

########################
# 5. Train / test split
########################
set.seed(462)

train_index <- sample(1:nrow(data), size = 0.8 * nrow(data))
df_train <- data[train_index, ]
df_test  <- data[-train_index, ]

dim(df_train)
dim(df_test)

############################################################
# PART A. EDA / VISUALISATION
############################################################

########################
# A1. Histogram of study hours
########################
ggplot(data, aes(x = study_hours_per_day)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "skyblue", color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Histogram of Study Hours Per Day",
    x = "Study Hours Per Day",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

########################
# A2. Histogram of productivity score
########################
ggplot(data, aes(x = productivity_score)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "tomato", color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Histogram of Productivity Score",
    x = "Productivity Score",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

########################
# A3. KDE / Density plot of study hours
########################
ggplot(data, aes(x = study_hours_per_day)) +
  geom_density(fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Density Plot of Study Hours Per Day",
    x = "Study Hours Per Day",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

########################
# A4. KDE / Density plot of productivity score
########################
ggplot(data, aes(x = productivity_score)) +
  geom_density(fill = "salmon", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Density Plot of Productivity Score",
    x = "Productivity Score",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

########################
# A5. Boxplot of productivity score
########################
ggplot(data, aes(y = productivity_score)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "orange", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Boxplot of Productivity Score",
    y = "Productivity Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

########################
# A6. Boxplot of productivity score by gender
########################
ggplot(data, aes(x = gender, y = productivity_score, fill = gender)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Productivity Score by Gender",
    x = "Gender",
    y = "Productivity Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

########################
# A7. Boxplot of productivity level groups by study hours
# Create study-hour groups for clearer boxplot
########################
data$study_hour_group <- cut(
  data$study_hours_per_day,
  breaks = c(-Inf, 2, 4, 6, 8, Inf),
  labels = c("<=2", "2-4", "4-6", "6-8", ">8")
)

ggplot(data, aes(x = study_hour_group, y = productivity_score, fill = study_hour_group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Productivity Score Across Study Hour Groups",
    x = "Study Hours Group",
    y = "Productivity Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

########################
# A8. Scatterplot: core relationship
########################
ggplot(data, aes(x = study_hours_per_day, y = productivity_score)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Study Hours Per Day vs Productivity Score",
    x = "Study Hours Per Day",
    y = "Productivity Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

########################
# A9. Scatterplot with color by gender
########################
ggplot(data, aes(x = study_hours_per_day, y = productivity_score, color = gender)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Study Hours vs Productivity Score by Gender",
    x = "Study Hours Per Day",
    y = "Productivity Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

########################
# A10. Density plot of productivity score by high/low productivity
########################
ggplot(data, aes(x = productivity_score, fill = high_productivity)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(
    title = "Density of Productivity Score by Binary Group",
    x = "Productivity Score",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

########################
# A11. Correlation matrix for numeric variables
########################
# Numeric variables only
numeric_data <- data %>%
  select(
    age,
    study_hours_per_day,
    sleep_hours,
    phone_usage_hours,
    social_media_hours,
    youtube_hours,
    gaming_hours,
    breaks_per_day,
    coffee_intake_mg,
    exercise_minutes,
    assignments_completed,
    attendance_percentage,
    stress_level,
    focus_score,
    final_grade,
    productivity_score
  )

# Correlation matrix
cor_matrix <- cor(numeric_data)

# Show more decimals
round(cor_matrix, 4)

corrplot(
  cor_matrix,
  method = "color",
  type = "upper",
  tl.col = "black",
  tl.cex = 0.7,
  addCoef.col = "black",
  number.cex = 0.5
)

########################
# A12. Pair plot for selected variables
########################
data %>%
  select(
    study_hours_per_day,
    productivity_score,
    focus_score,
    attendance_percentage,
    assignments_completed,
    sleep_hours,
    stress_level
  ) %>%
  ggpairs()

############################################################
# PART B. SIMPLE LINEAR REGRESSION
############################################################

########################
# B1. Fit simple linear regression
########################
slr_model <- lm(productivity_score ~ study_hours_per_day, data = df_train)

summary(slr_model)

########################
# B2. Predictions on test set
########################
slr_pred <- predict(slr_model, newdata = df_test)

########################
# B3. Evaluate SLR
########################
slr_mse  <- mean((df_test$productivity_score - slr_pred)^2)
slr_rmse <- sqrt(slr_mse)
slr_r2_train <- summary(slr_model)$r.squared

slr_mse
slr_rmse
slr_r2_train

########################
# B4. Actual vs predicted plot
########################
ggplot(data.frame(actual = df_test$productivity_score, predicted = slr_pred),
       aes(x = actual, y = predicted)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "purple") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Simple Linear Regression: Actual vs Predicted",
    x = "Actual Productivity Score",
    y = "Predicted Productivity Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

########################
# B5. Diagnostic plots
########################
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(slr_model)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

############################################################
# PART C. MULTIPLE LINEAR REGRESSION
############################################################

########################
# C1. Fit multiple linear regression
########################
mlr_model <- lm(
  productivity_score ~ study_hours_per_day +
    focus_score +
    attendance_percentage +
    assignments_completed +
    sleep_hours +
    stress_level,
  data = df_train
)

summary(mlr_model)

########################
# C2. Predictions on test set
########################
mlr_pred <- predict(mlr_model, newdata = df_test)

########################
# C3. Evaluate MLR
########################
mlr_mse  <- mean((df_test$productivity_score - mlr_pred)^2)
mlr_rmse <- sqrt(mlr_mse)
mlr_r2_train <- summary(mlr_model)$r.squared
mlr_adj_r2_train <- summary(mlr_model)$adj.r.squared

mlr_mse
mlr_rmse
mlr_r2_train
mlr_adj_r2_train

########################
# C4. Actual vs predicted plot
########################
ggplot(data.frame(actual = df_test$productivity_score, predicted = mlr_pred),
       aes(x = actual, y = predicted)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "darkgreen") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Multiple Linear Regression: Actual vs Predicted",
    x = "Actual Productivity Score",
    y = "Predicted Productivity Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

########################
# C5. Diagnostic plots
########################
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(mlr_model)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

############################################################
# PART D. KNN REGRESSION
############################################################
# IMPORTANT:
# Since productivity_score is continuous, we use KNN REGRESSION.
# Do NOT use confusionMatrix() here.

########################
# D1. Select predictors for KNN
########################
knn_train_x <- df_train %>%
  select(
    study_hours_per_day,
    focus_score,
    attendance_percentage,
    assignments_completed,
    sleep_hours,
    stress_level
  )

knn_test_x <- df_test %>%
  select(
    study_hours_per_day,
    focus_score,
    attendance_percentage,
    assignments_completed,
    sleep_hours,
    stress_level
  )

knn_train_y <- df_train$productivity_score
knn_test_y  <- df_test$productivity_score

########################
# D2. Scale predictors using training set parameters
########################
preproc <- preProcess(knn_train_x, method = c("center", "scale"))

knn_train_x_scaled <- predict(preproc, knn_train_x)
knn_test_x_scaled  <- predict(preproc, knn_test_x)

########################
# D3. Fit KNN regression with one k value first
########################
k_value <- 15

knn_model_15 <- knn.reg(
  train = knn_train_x_scaled,
  test  = knn_test_x_scaled,
  y     = knn_train_y,
  k     = k_value
)

knn_pred_15 <- knn_model_15$pred

########################
# D4. Evaluate KNN regression
########################
knn_mse_15  <- mean((knn_test_y - knn_pred_15)^2)
knn_rmse_15 <- sqrt(knn_mse_15)

knn_mse_15
knn_rmse_15

########################
# D5. Compare several k values
########################
k_grid <- c(3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 21, 25, 31)

knn_results <- data.frame(
  k = integer(),
  mse = numeric(),
  rmse = numeric()
)

for (k in k_grid) {
  knn_fit <- knn.reg(
    train = knn_train_x_scaled,
    test  = knn_test_x_scaled,
    y     = knn_train_y,
    k     = k
  )

  pred_k <- knn_fit$pred
  mse_k  <- mean((knn_test_y - pred_k)^2)
  rmse_k <- sqrt(mse_k)

  knn_results <- rbind(
    knn_results,
    data.frame(k = k, mse = mse_k, rmse = rmse_k)
  )
}

knn_results

########################
# D6. Best k
########################
best_k_row <- knn_results[which.min(knn_results$rmse), ]
best_k_row

best_k <- best_k_row$k

########################
# D7. Fit final KNN regression with best k
########################
knn_final <- knn.reg(
  train = knn_train_x_scaled,
  test  = knn_test_x_scaled,
  y     = knn_train_y,
  k     = best_k
)

knn_pred_final <- knn_final$pred

knn_final_mse  <- mean((knn_test_y - knn_pred_final)^2)
knn_final_rmse <- sqrt(knn_final_mse)

knn_final_mse
knn_final_rmse

########################
# D8. Plot K vs RMSE
########################
ggplot(knn_results, aes(x = k, y = rmse)) +
  geom_line(color = "blue") +
  geom_point(size = 2, color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "KNN Regression: RMSE for Different k Values",
    x = "k",
    y = "RMSE"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

########################
# D9. Actual vs predicted plot for final KNN
########################
ggplot(data.frame(actual = knn_test_y, predicted = knn_pred_final),
       aes(x = actual, y = predicted)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "brown") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = paste("KNN Regression (k =", best_k, "): Actual vs Predicted"),
    x = "Actual Productivity Score",
    y = "Predicted Productivity Score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

############################################################
# PART E. OPTIONAL LOGISTIC REGRESSION
############################################################
# This is not the main model for your question.
# We only include it because you previously asked for logistic regression.
# Here, the target is High vs Low productivity.

########################
# E1. Logistic regression model
########################
logit_model <- glm(
  high_productivity ~ study_hours_per_day +
    focus_score +
    attendance_percentage +
    assignments_completed +
    sleep_hours +
    stress_level,
  data = df_train,
  family = binomial
)

summary(logit_model)

########################
# E2. Predict probabilities on test set
########################
logit_prob <- predict(logit_model, newdata = df_test, type = "response")

########################
# E3. Convert probabilities to class labels
########################
logit_pred <- ifelse(logit_prob > 0.5, "High", "Low")
logit_pred <- factor(logit_pred, levels = c("Low", "High"))


#Actual classes
logit_actual <- df_test$high_productivity

#E5. Confusion matrix
confusionMatrix(logit_pred, logit_actual, positive = "High")


#PART F. MODEL COMPARISON
comparison_table <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Simple Linear Regression", "Multiple Linear Regression", "KNN Regression"),
  Test_MSE = c(slr_mse, mlr_mse, knn_final_mse),
  Test_RMSE = c(slr_rmse, mlr_rmse, knn_final_rmse)
)

comparison_table

cat("Main Research Question:\n")
cat("What is the relationship between study_hours_per_day and productivity_score?\n")
cat("Can study_hours_per_day predict productivity_score?\n\n")

cat("SLR coefficient for study_hours_per_day:\n")
print(coef(slr_model))

cat("\nMLR coefficients:\n")
print(coef(mlr_model))

cat("\nBest k for KNN regression:\n")
print(best_k)

cat("\nModel comparison:\n")
print(comparison_table)
```




```{r}
# Create standardized training data
df_train_std <- df_train %>%
  mutate(
    productivity_score = scale(productivity_score)[, 1],
    study_hours_per_day = scale(study_hours_per_day)[, 1],
    focus_score = scale(focus_score)[, 1],
    attendance_percentage = scale(attendance_percentage)[, 1],
    assignments_completed = scale(assignments_completed)[, 1],
    sleep_hours = scale(sleep_hours)[, 1],
    stress_level = scale(stress_level)[, 1]
  )

# Fit standardized multiple linear regression
mlr_model_std <- lm(
  productivity_score ~ study_hours_per_day +
    focus_score +
    attendance_percentage +
    assignments_completed +
    sleep_hours +
    stress_level,
  data = df_train_std
)

summary(mlr_model_std)

std_coef <- summary(mlr_model_std)$coefficients
std_coef

std_coef_table <- data.frame(
  Variable = rownames(std_coef),
  Estimate = std_coef[, "Estimate"],
  Std_Error = std_coef[, "Std. Error"],
  t_value = std_coef[, "t value"],
  p_value = std_coef[, "Pr(>|t|)"]
)

std_coef_table


std_coef_table_no_intercept <- std_coef_table %>%
  filter(Variable != "(Intercept)") %>%
  mutate(abs_estimate = abs(Estimate)) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs_estimate))

std_coef_table_no_intercept

ggplot(std_coef_table_no_intercept,
       aes(x = reorder(Variable, abs_estimate), y = abs_estimate)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Feature Importance Based on Standardized Coefficients",
    x = "Feature",
    y = "Absolute Standardized Coefficient"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()



```
